metadata.title: How to set up Claude Cowork.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Cowork lives in the desktop app between Chat and Code; it spawns many Claudes and saves a real file to your disk. The common setup points Cowork at one folder holding an about-me file and an output folder it reads before answering. That setup breaks: folders leak stale outputs into new answers, and nobody maintains the about-me file, so it rots. Shared folders multiply both problems, so the replacement reduces everything to two things: Skills and Projects.

artifactLines: Cowork lives in the desktop app between Chat and Code; it spawns many Claudes and saves a real file to your disk.
The common setup points Cowork at one folder holding an about-me file and an output folder it reads before answering.
That setup breaks: folders leak stale outputs into new answers, and nobody maintains the about-me file, so it rots.
Shared folders multiply both problems, so the replacement reduces everything to two things: Skills and Projects.

beats.narration_text: I finally get why the shared-folder setup breaks — stale outputs poison new answers and the about-me file rots. But I want to actually migrate to the Skills-and-Projects replacement instead of just nodding along. Walk me through it: (1) show me a minimal Skill file that captures my personal context the way an about-me file used to, (2) explain exactly how a Project keeps my outputs isolated from a teammate's so the folder can't leak, and (3) give me a checklist to retire my old folder without losing anything I still need.

props.segment: How to Set Up Claude Cowork.
props.command: I finally get why the shared-folder setup breaks — stale outputs poison new answers and the about-me file rots. But I want to actually migrate to the Skills-and-Projects replacement instead of just nodding along. Walk me through it: (1) show me a minimal Skill file that captures my personal context the way an about-me file used to, (2) explain exactly how a Project keeps my outputs isolated from a teammate's so the folder can't leak, and (3) give me a checklist to retire my old folder without losing anything I still need.

beats.narration_text: Start from zero. Cowork lives in the Claude desktop app, in the tab between Chat and Code. You need a Pro account — twenty dollars a month minimum, higher tiers cost more. The core difference from regular Claude: Cowork doesn't give you one answer. It spawns multiple Claudes to plan, search, and build in parallel. One prompt can run for thirty minutes. It makes the actual file — the real PDF, the real spreadsheet — and saves it to your computer. That's the whole thing. Many Claudes, one output, on your disk.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B01.png (screenshot of the Claude desktop app with the Cowork tab visible and active, showing the tab layout between Chat and Code)

beats.narration_text: Start with the older setup that spread widely online. A folder on your computer. Inside it: an about-me file with your personal context, an output folder for Cowork's deliverables. You point Cowork to that folder when you start a session and it reads everything before answering. It works. It works for an individual, but enterprise workshops expose where the setup falls apart.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot of the old Cowork folder structure — about-me files and output files inside a computer folder, or the Cowork folder picker)

beats.narration_text: Two failure modes, both real. First: folders leak. Even when Cowork is told not to read the output folder, it may still pull in stale results, old drafts, and wrong versions. Old outputs poison the new answer. Second: files rot. Nobody maintains an about-me file indefinitely. It starts useful and becomes a bottleneck. And when a team shares a folder, both problems multiply. Someone else's outputs contaminate your session. Someone else's about-me file overwrites your context. The architecture that works for one person breaks at two.

artifactLines: Folders leak — old outputs sneak into context even when you say not to read them.
Files rot — about-me files need maintenance nobody actually does.
Solo: you survive both. Team: both failures multiply across every user.
The architecture is fine for one. It breaks the moment you share.

beats.narration_text: The replacement. The replacement reduces the system to two things. Skills and Projects. A Skill is a capability — a reusable set of instructions you call with a slash command, like /linkedin-post or /meeting-summary. It travels into any chat. You don't maintain it; you write it once and it works. A Project is a place — a persistent workspace where your files and instructions stay loaded across sessions and your team can join. No about-me files to rot. No output folders to leak. If you take one concept from this breakdown: a Skill is what you call, a Project is where you go.

artifactLines: Skill: a slash-command capability. Write once, call anywhere, portable.
Project: a persistent workspace. Files + instructions stay loaded. Team-shareable.
No about-me files to maintain. No output folders to accidentally read.
Solo: folders are still fine. Team: drop them entirely.
props.sparkLine: Call a skill. Go to a project.

beats.narration_text: A Skill is just a file. A name, a description that tells Claude when to reach for it, and the instructions underneath. You load it once — left sidebar, Customize, Skills, the plus button, Create skill — and from that point, typing /skill-name in any chat activates it. What changes: you write the judgment down once, and every future conversation inherits it. Your team can share skills. Teams can share custom skill sets for repeatable onboarding. A public /how-to Skill demonstrates the pattern: package beginner guidance as a reusable capability.

props.title: example-skill/SKILL.md
props.code:
---
name: weekly-brief
description: > Turn raw notes or a Granola transcript into a structured weekly brief. Use when I type /weekly-brief or ask for a brief from meeting notes.
--- # Format: three sections — Done, Decisions, Next.
# Length: under one page. No bullets longer than 15 words.
# Tone: direct. No 'in today's world'. No 'leverage'.

beats.narration_text: A Project is where the persistent context lives. You create it from the left sidebar, add files — your company overview, your brand voice, a reference doc — and write custom instructions for that workspace. From then on, every session inside that Project starts with all of it pre-loaded. You don't re-upload anything. Your team members join the Project and get the same context. This is how enterprises run it: one shared Project, specific Skills mapped to recurring tasks, Opus or Fable for complex turns, Sonnet for routine execution.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B06.png (screenshot of a Claude Project showing the left-sidebar Projects menu, the files and custom instructions panel, and the share/collaborators control)

beats.narration_text: One more thing: model selection inside Cowork matters. For complex, high-stakes work — market research, long document analysis, strategy — select Opus or Fable when it's available. Not because Sonnet can't do it, but because accuracy on a complex task has a real cost if it's wrong. For routine tasks inside the same session, Sonnet is fine. The model selector is in the bottom-right of the Cowork interface. A strong default is Opus with Adaptive thinking turned on. Fable when it's available and the task earns it.

artifactLines: Opus + Adaptive thinking: a strong default. Good for anything complex.
Fable-5: use when available, for high-stakes planning and hard reasoning.
Sonnet: routine execution, drafts, formatting — most of what you actually do.
Selector is bottom-right in Cowork. Change it per task, not per session.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Don't start by rebuilding a folder. Start by turning your most-repeated task into a Skill. Paste this, and Cowork walks you through it.

props.segment: How to Set Up Claude Cowork.
props.command: I want to build my first Claude Skill from my most-repeated weekly task. Ask me 5 questions to understand the task, then write me a complete SKILL.md file I can load directly into Claude. End with one sentence on which Project to put it in.

beats.narration_text: How to set up Claude Cowork.

props.title: How to set up Claude Cowork.
props.subline: Skills to call · Projects to share
